source_refs.html: /Users/toy/English/躺着学/躺着学阅读/6815_Passage 3 The value of literary prizes.html

passages.title: The value of literary prizes
passages.source_html:
The value of literary prizes


A


It is a central paradox of literary writing that true greatness only becomes apparent over time, and yet that the judgements of the future are substantially dependent on what the present chooses to publish, publicise and preserve. Viewed from the pinnacles of hindsight literary history looks like a stately procession of great texts. A snapshot taken at any particular moment, however, reveals a far messier business: one clogged with readers, writers, commercial obligations, prejudices and misconceptions. All those enduring works that collectively form a standard by which we judge others are busily being forged or maintained within that snapshot. And somewhere close to the heart of this business lies one of the most ancient and contentious of all artistic institutions: the literary prize.


B


The ancient Greeks founded the idea of the literary prize. In fact, the Greek calendar was full of formal contests, most of which were either poetical, athletic or some combination of the two; the competitive singing of dithyrambs – choruses of praise to the god Dionysus – was a kind of literary sport, with groups of up to 50 men or boys moving in stately circles and declaiming verse in order to win the honour of coming first. It might look comical to our eyes, but this was art of the most serious kind. To my mind, just as sporting spectacles today offer people the thrill of watching the human body pushed toward its limits, so the quest for supremacy in words made the deepest concerns of the mind accessible to a wide audience in classical Greece.


C


After the classical era, the importance of prizes diminished. Later, in medieval Europe, aristocratic patrons and learned societies singled out the most promising writers and commissioned and rewarded them. It was only at the start of the 20th century that the first truly modern literary prize arrived. Alfred Nobel, the inventor of dynamite, bequeathed an immense sum, equivalent to US $186m today, to fund five international prizes in the fields of literature, chemistry, physics, medicine and peace (prompted, it is said, by the guilt he felt when a French newspaper described him as a ‘merchant of death’). In 1901, the first Nobel prizes were announced. Crucially, they were awarded not by patrons or clubs, but by impartial and authoritative committees – something as close to an absolute and enduring standard as the modern world could find. Their impact was instant, and the results were reported in over 100 newspapers worldwide. Like the newly reborn Olympic movement (founded in 1896), here was an annual competition that would deliver a stream of victory headlines.


D


Nobel imitators soon came thick and fast: the Goncourt and Femina prizes in France, the Pulitzer in America, and many more. However, as the years went by neither the gradual expansion of the universities nor the increasing number of awards seemed to be infusing the general reader with an overwhelming regard for great literary works. For example, in 1955 the recently formed British Crime Writers’ Association started an annual prize for the best crime novel; in the same year, there was an annual award for Science Fiction. What was emerging was a complex system of patronage whose value was open to dispute. Perhaps the most familiar example of this is Britain’s Booker prize (now the Man Booker). Where the Nobel had aimed at rewarding ‘an ideal direction’ in literature, this award intended to stimulate interest ‘in serious British fiction as a whole’. The people behind it had clearly learned something from the bestseller market. The Booker would aim at catching the imagination of the press by establishing an explicitly sporting-style atmosphere: a shortlist of authors, a high-profile panel of judges, an exciting victor. Above all, though, what came to impress was the fact that by the 1990s, winners could regularly expect to shift over 500,000 copies.


E


Yet there is a pale glow of illusion surrounding the wilder claims made for prizes. Winners can prosper, but no victory guarantees vast sales. Most importantly, there is still no substitute for word of mouth. In 2007, The Reluctant Fundamentalist by Mohsin Hamid lost out to Kiran Desai’s. The Inheritance of Loss in the Man Booker, but considerably outsold it, becoming one of the best-performing literary novels of the year. It was also a far more ambitious and exciting book. Prizes grant opportunities, but their winners remain at the mercy of the reading public. And the bottom line is that this public is ill – served by much of the current marketplace of overlapping awards and those books that are manufactured to claim them – sensitive, trendy tracts of needlessly effortful prose whose elegant openings so impress some juries.


F


If, however, we are willing to have a little more faith in the hunger that’s still out there for fresh, awakening words, there remain causes for hope. Literature should take a step towards this audience – should wind down some of the media circus, shed its timid obsession with narrow categories, and learn a little more from the worlds both of genre fiction (where much that is most exciting in contemporary writing is taking place) and grassroots prizes like the Man Booker. But we should thin out the other awards, and spend some money instead on competitions that will get writers’ first books into print. We should restore proper expertise to juries, and then – and only then – open the door to science fiction, crime, thrillers, horror and everything else that’s out there racking up sales.

groups.title: Questions 27-40
groups.source_html:
Questions 27 – 30


Choose the correct answer.

27

What is the writer’s main point about great literary works in the first paragraph?

正确答案： C. Their quality is not always immediately recognised.

Q27, C

定位关键词：

great literary works

原文：

A段, “It is a central paradox of literary writing that true greatness only becomes apparent over time, and yet that the judgements of the future are substantially dependent on what the present chooses to publish, publicise and preserve.”

解析：

原文提到了一个悖论，即伟大的作品只会随着时间的推移而得到认可。

28

The writer compares Greek poetry contests to sports events because of

正确答案： A. their impact on spectators.

Q28, A

定位关键词：

sports events

原文：

B段, “To my mind, just as sporting spectacles today offer people the thrill of watching the human body pushed toward its limits, so the quest for supremacy in words made the deepest concerns of the mind accessible to a wide audience in classical Greece.”

解析：

原文将诗歌竞赛比作体育赛事，因为它们使广大观众能够让观众感受到人类思想的力量

同义替换：

sports events =sporting spectacles

29

What point is the writer making in the fourth paragraph?

正确答案： D. More awards did not lead to a greater appreciation of literary merit.

Q29, D

定位关键词：

the fourth paragraph

原文：

D段, “However, as the years went by neither the gradual expansion of the universities nor the increasing number of awards seemed to be infusing the general reader with an overwhelming regard for great literary works.”

解析：

原文提到尽管文学奖项数量不断增加，但并没有导致人们对优秀文学作品的普遍欣赏。

30

What are we told about the development of the Man Booker prize?

正确答案： C. The organisers knew how to generate interest in it.

Q30, C

定位关键词：

Man Booker prize

原文：

D段, “Where the Nobel had aimed at rewarding ‘an ideal direction’ in literature, this award intended to stimulate interest ‘in serious British fiction as a whole’. The people behind it had clearly learned something from the bestseller market. The Booker would aim at catching the imagination of the press by establishing an explicitly sporting-style atmosphere: a shortlist of authors, a high-profile panel of judges, an exciting victor.”

解析：

原文提到布克奖知道如何抓住媒体的想象力，激发人们的兴趣。

同义替换：

generate interest=stimulate interest

Questions 31 – 35


Choose YES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer, choose NO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer, or choose NOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this.

31

The founders of the Man Booker prize rejected high-profile commercialization of its prize.

正确答案： B. NO

Q31, NO

定位关键词：

Man Booker prize

原文：

D段，”The people behind it had clearly learned something from the bestseller market. The Booker would aim at catching the imagination of the press by establishing an explicitly sporting-style atmosphere: a shortlist of authors, a high-profile panel of judges, an exciting victor. Above all, though, what came to impress was the fact that by the 1990s, winners could regularly expect to shift over 500,000 copies.”

解析：

文章提到Man Booker prize的创立者模仿畅销书市场，将这个奖项打造成体育比赛形式来吸引参赛者。获胜者通常可以卖出50万份的书。这与题目中的反对商业化想矛盾。

同义替换：

the people behind it=the founders

32

The judges of the 2007 Man Booker prize came to regret their choice of winning title.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

Q32, NOT GIVEN

定位关键词：

2007

解析：

原文并没有提到评委们后悔自己的选择。

33

Some authors deliberately write in an exaggerated style which they think will attract judges.

正确答案： A. YES

Q33, YES

定位关键词：

attract judges

原文：

E段, “And the bottom line is that this public is ill-served by much of the current marketplace of overlapping awards and those books that are manufactured to claim them–sensitive, trendy tracts of needlessly effortful prose whose elegant openings so impress some juries.”

解析：

原文提到一些作者开始创作出哗众取宠的作品，他们认为这样的开头会给评委留下深刻的印象。

同义替换：

attract=impress

exaggerated style=sensitive, trendy tracts of needlessly effortful prose

34

There should be a reduction in the number of literary awards.

正确答案： A. YES

Q34, YES

定位关键词：

reduction，literary awards


原文：

F段, “But we should thin out the other awards, and spend some money instead on competitions that will get writers’ first books into print.”

解析：

原文中作者建议文学界应该减少其他奖项，把钱花在能让作家第一本书出版的比赛上。

同义替换：

thin out=reduction

35

Juries of literary awards should consist of writers rather than critics.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

Q35, NOT GIVEN

解析：

原文并没有提到陪审团的理想组成情况。

Questions 36 – 40


Complete each sentence with the correct ending. Choose the correct ending and move it into the gap.

Options (Word Bank):
A. were considered to be less significant than previously.
B. were intended to be a boost to public education.
C. were covered by the international press.
D. were supported by wealthy people.
E. were felt to be out of touch with their time.
F. were presented for many different categories of literature.
G. were presented to teams for oral performance of sacred works.
Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
36

In ancient Greece, prizes________
✅ 正确答案：G

审题: 题目问古希腊的奖项是如何颁发的。


定位关键信息: ancient Greece


对应原文: B段提到“The ancient Greeks founded the idea of the literary prize. In fact, the Greek calendar was full of formal contests, most of which were either poetical, athletic or some combination of the two; the competitive singing of dithyrambs – choruses of praise to the god Dionysus – was a kind of literary sport, with groups of up to 50 men or boys moving in stately circles and declaiming verse in order to win the honour of coming first .”


解题思路: 原文提到古希腊人开创了文学竞赛，50人或更少的男子或男孩团体围成一个庄严的圈子，高声吟诵诗歌verse，以争取头筹的荣誉，对应G选项。


同义替换: “teams”对应“groups”，“oral performance”对应“declaiming”，“sacred works”对应“verse”
37

In the post-classical period, literary prizes________
✅ 正确答案：A

审题: 题目询问后古典时期文学奖项的变化。


定位关键信息: post-classical period


对应原文: C段,“After the classical era, the importance of prizes diminished.”


解题思路: 原文提到在古典时代之后，奖项的意义低于之前。


同义替换: “post-classical period”对应“After the classical era”,“less significant than previously” 对应“importance... diminished”
38

In medieval Europe, talented writers________
✅ 正确答案：D

审题: 题目问中世纪欧洲的天才作家。


定位关键信息: medieval Europe, talented writers


对应原文: C段，“Later, in medieval Europe, aristocratic patrons and learned societies singled out the most promising writers and commissioned and rewarded them.”


解题思路: 原文提到有钱人支持有潜力的作家。


同义替换: “supported”对应“commissioned”和“rewarded”，“wealthy people”对应“patrons”，“promising”对应“talented”
39

The first results issued by the Nobel foundation________
✅ 正确答案：C

审题: 题目询问诺贝尔基金会的首批结果。


定位关键信息: first results, Nobel foundation


对应原文: C段，“In 1901, the first Nobel prizes were announced. Crucially, they were awarded not by patrons or clubs, but by impartial and authoritative committees – something as close to an absolute and enduring standard as the modern world could find. Their impact was instant, and the results were reported in over 100 newspapers worldwide.”


解题思路: 原文提到这些奖项受到国际媒体的广泛报道。


同义替换: “Nobel prizes”对应“Nobel foundation”，“issued”对应“announced”，“international”对应“worldwide”，“press”对应“newspapers”
40

After the establishment of the Nobel prizes, other awards________
✅ 正确答案：F

审题: 题目询问诺贝尔奖成立后的其他奖项发展情况。


定位关键信息: other awards


对应原文: D段, “For example, in 1955 the recently formed British Crime Writers’ Association started an annual prize for the best crime novel; in the same year, there was an annual award for Science Fiction.”


解题思路: 原文提到诺贝尔奖之后出现了特定类型的文学奖项。


同义替换: “many different categories”对应“crime novel”和“Science Fiction”
